title: Persist copy-mode mark in pane history
author: leohenon
body:
Could the existing `set-mark` survive leaving and re-entering copy mode, remaining attached to the marked content in the pane history?

This was previously requested in #2280 (https://github.com/tmux/tmux/issues/2280#issuecomment-661640540). The mark would be cleared if its line is evicted, the history is cleared, or the pane is reset.

I’m interested in implementing this using pane/grid metadata rather than copy-mode coordinates, so scrolling and reflow carry the mark with the content. Would this behavior and approach be acceptable?

title: Reply to tmux's own OSC 52 clipboard query can be flushed into the active pane as literal keystrokes (partial-key delay guard ignores TTY_OSC52QUERY)
author: jacksonedmondscanva
body:
### Issue description

When tmux itself requests the terminal clipboard (`refresh-client -l`, i.e. an OSC 52 query), the reply is parsed by `tty_keys_next()`. If the reply arrives split across reads, the partially received sequence is held on the partial-key timer, which uses plain `escape-time`. The "increasing delay (active query)" guard in `tty_keys_next()` (tty-keys.c around line 980) extends the delay to 500ms for outstanding DA1/DA2/XDA and fg/bg colour queries and for pane-originated `input_requests`, but it does not consult `TTY_OSC52QUERY`, the flag set by `tty_clipboard_query()`. So with a small `escape-time`, if the rest of the reply does not arrive before the timer fires, the partial `\033]52;c;<base64...>` is delivered to the active pane as literal keystrokes: `M-]` followed by the base64 of the clipboard, typed one character at a time.

Since the payload is the base64 of the system clipboard, this is also a mild security concern: clipboard contents (possibly a password) end up injected into whatever application has focus, and are trivially decodable from scrollback.

I hit this with `escape-time 0` (clamped to 1ms) plus a `client-focus-in` hook running `refresh-client -l`, where roughly 1 in 20-50 window refocuses pasted a base64 blob into the focused TUI. I appreciate `escape-time 0` is an aggressive setting that makes the race easy to lose, but the reply to a query tmux knowingly has in flight (it holds a 5s `clipboard_timer` for it) arguably should not depend on the human-input disambiguation timeout at all, which seems to be exactly the reasoning behind the existing guard covering every other self-initiated query type. The OSC 52 reply is also the only reply with unbounded, user-controlled length, so it is the one most likely to split across reads.

#### Reproduction

1. `tmux -Ltest -f/dev/null new`
2. `tmux -Ltest set -s escape-time 0`
3. Put a few KB of text in the system clipboard (bigger clipboard = wider race window).
4. In a pane, run something that shows typed input (e.g. `cat`), then repeatedly run `refresh-client -l` from another pane or a hook while the server has work to do (redrawing on focus changes is what triggered it for me; a terminal that is slow to answer the clipboard query also widens the window).
5. Intermittently, `52;c;` plus a long base64 string appears as typed input in the active pane. With `-vv` logs, the failing case shows `partial key` for the `\033]52;...` prefix followed by the timer firing and the sequence going through the literal-key path, instead of `tty_keys_clipboard` completing.

The intermittency is a read-chunking race: if the whole reply lands in one `evbuffer_read`, `tty_keys_clipboard()` returns 0 and everything is fine; if it splits, the timer from the first chunk is never re-armed when more data arrives, so the entire remainder must arrive within `escape-time` of the first chunk.

#### Suggested fix

Include the outstanding-clipboard-query flag in the delay extension in `tty_keys_next()`:

```diff
-	if ((tty->flags & (TTY_WAITFG|TTY_WAITBG) ||
+	if ((tty->flags & (TTY_WAITFG|TTY_WAITBG|TTY_OSC52QUERY|TTY_WINSIZEQUERY) ||
 	    (tty->flags & TTY_ALL_REQUEST_FLAGS) != TTY_ALL_REQUEST_FLAGS) ||
 	    !TAILQ_EMPTY(&c->input_requests)) {
```

`TTY_OSC52QUERY` cannot simply be added to `TTY_ALL_REQUEST_FLAGS` because the polarity is inverted: the HAVEDA flags mean "response received" while `TTY_OSC52QUERY` means "query outstanding". The delay stays bounded since the flag is cleared on reply or by the existing 5s `clipboard_timer`. `TTY_WINSIZEQUERY` has the same exposure in principle (also absent from the guard), though its reply is a short CSI so the race is much harder to hit; included above for consistency, happy to drop it.

A complementary hardening would be re-arming the partial-key timer when new data arrives for an incomplete sequence, but the flag addition alone closes the observed bug.

### Required information

* tmux version (`tmux -V`): tmux 3.7b (code inspection says the issue is unchanged on current master)
* Platform (`uname -sp`): Darwin arm (macOS, Apple Silicon)
* Terminal in use: Ghostty 1.3.1 (`clipboard-read = allow`)
* $TERM inside tmux: `tmux-256color`
* $TERM outside tmux: `xterm-ghostty`
* Logs: the failure is intermittent (read-chunking race described above); in `-vv` logs the failing case shows `partial key` for the `\033]52;...` prefix, then the timer firing and the sequence taking the literal-key path instead of `tty_keys_clipboard` completing.

title: Security contact
author: randomdude
body: We believe we've found a command injection flaw in tmux, and would like to report it privately, rather than via the public mailinglist. Could we please have a contact email to do so? Thanks.

title: escape delay doesn't get set correctly until escape is pressed a couple of times
author: yousef8192
body:
### Issue description

I have `set -sg escape-time 50` only in .tmux.conf
When I open a new tmux session, the escape delay is set to the default and not 50 and in order to make it 50 I have to press escape a couple of times (about 3 times but sometimes it changes)
This issue isn't present in all terminals, for an instance it is present in terminator, lxterminal, urxvt, and st but not in xterm.

### Required information

* tmux version (`tmux -V`).
tmux 3.7b
* Platform (`uname -sp`).
Linux unkown
* Terminal in use (xterm, rxvt, etc).
Terminals I tested that have the issue are terminator, lxterminal, urxvt, and st 
Terminals I tested that don't have the issue are xterm
* $TERM *inside* tmux (`echo $TERM`).
tmux-256color (this output is from st terminal)
* $TERM *outside* tmux (`echo $TERM`).
st-256color (this output is from st terminal)
* Logs from tmux (`tmux kill-server; tmux -vv new`).
[server exited]

title: grid_check_is_clear assertion failure (SIGABRT) entering copy-mode on macOS (master)
author: JonesHong
body:
### Issue description

Following up on #5314, where @nicm noted the macOS grid checks stay in until this bug is found and that `grid_check_is_clear` is the important one — we hit that assertion twice in normal desktop use and have crash reports.

**Build:** tmux master via Homebrew `--HEAD`, commit `9180356`, built 2026-07-06 (`tmux -V` reported `next-3.8`)
**Platform:** macOS 26.5.1 (25F80), Apple Silicon (Mac mini)

Two SIGABRTs four days apart (2026-07-06 20:57, 2026-07-10 09:51) with an **identical faulting stack** — the assert fires while entering copy-mode, when the pane screen is cloned:

```
#0 libsystem_kernel.dylib!__pthread_kill+8
#1 libsystem_pthread.dylib!pthread_kill+296
#2 libsystem_c.dylib!abort+148
#3 libsystem_c.dylib!__assert_rtn+284
#4 tmux!grid_check_is_clear.cold.4+36      (second crash: .cold.5)
#5 tmux!grid_check_is_clear+144            (second crash: +148)
#6 tmux!grid_create+96
#7 tmux!screen_init+32
#8 tmux!window_copy_clone_screen+220
#9 tmux!window_copy_init+72
#10 tmux!window_pane_set_mode+196
#11 tmux!cmd_copy_mode_exec+384
#12 tmux!cmdq_next+844
#13 tmux!server_loop+80
```

Both reports carry the ASI annotation `libsystem_c.dylib: crashed on child side of fork pre-exec` (verbatim; leaving interpretation to you).

**Workload context** (in case it helps narrow the search): this server sees a lot of scripted layout churn — windows created by scripts (`new-window`, several `split-window -l <cells>` in quick succession, explicit `resize-window -x -y`), `window-size manual`, hooks that immediately `resize-window` new windows again, and panes running full-screen TUIs producing heavy output. Copy-mode is entered frequently for scrollback; per the stack, the abort happens at copy-mode entry, so the grid presumably went inconsistent some time earlier during the layout churn.

Possibly related: on 2026-07-12 the same build's server exited abruptly twice (no crash report, socket left behind), each time within a minute of a scripted layout rebuild. Consistent with a `fatal()` exit path, but we could not confirm it is the same root cause.

We have since moved this machine back to 3.7b, so I can't provide `-vv` logs of the crash itself. The two full `.ips` crash reports are available on request, and I'm happy to run diagnostics or an instrumented build if that helps.

title: Key input deadlocks when a key binding runs run-shell that creates a floating pane and waits for it
author: junegunn
body:
### Issue description

When a key binding invokes a blocking `run-shell` whose command creates a floating pane and then waits for the pane to finish, the invoking client stops receiving all key input, including the prefix key, until the run-shell command is terminated externally. If the program in the floating pane needs keyboard input to exit, this is a deadlock: the program never receives a key, so run-shell never returns, so keys are never processed.

The server and other clients are unaffected. Buffered keys are delivered once the run-shell command finishes.

`display-popup -E` in the same context does not have this problem; popup input keeps working while the run-shell item is pending.

### Required information

* tmux 3.7b
* macOS 15 (Darwin 25.3.0)
* default configuration (`tmux -f /dev/null`) plus the two bindings below

### Steps to reproduce

    tmux -L test -f /dev/null new-session -d
    tmux -L test bind-key 0 run-shell 'tmux display-popup -E "sh -c \"read line\""'
    tmux -L test bind-key 1 run-shell 'tmux new-pane "sh -c \"read line; tmux wait-for -S done\""; tmux wait-for done'
    tmux -L test attach

Press `C-b 0`. A popup running `read` appears; while run-shell is pending, the popup receives keys, and typing a line closes it and unblocks the client.

Press `C-b 1`. A floating pane running `read` appears. Typing anything has no effect, and `C-b d` does not detach. From another terminal:

    tmux -L test wait-for -S done

unblocks the client; the buffered keys are then delivered (the typed line reaches `read` and closes the pane, and a buffered `C-b d` detaches).

To reproduce a second time, kill the test server first. Once the pane's `wait-for -S done` fires with no waiter, the channel stays woken, so the next `C-b 1` returns from `wait-for done` immediately and does not block.

### Expected behavior

Not sure what the intended behavior is. Since floating panes are a new alternative to popups, it may be surprising that keys are delivered to a popup but not to a floating pane in the same situation. If the behavior is intended, a note in the manual for run-shell or new-pane might help; the resulting state looks like a complete freeze of tmux to the user.

title: The use of the "¦" character in status-left/right causes seg-fault in tmux running under Cygwin
author: jmtysonjr
body:
### Issue description

Built pre-release 3.7c tmux from source using " ./configure --prefix=/usr/local/bin --with-TERM=tmux-256color --enable-utf8proc".

The use of the "¦" character in status-left/right causes seg-fault in tmux running under Cygwin running under Windows 10 Pro 22H2 x64.

cat << EOF > ~/.tmux.conf
set-option -g status-left ' #{=20:session_name} ¦ '
set-option -g status-left-length 24
EOF

### Required information

Please provide the following information. These are **required**. Note that bug reports without logs may be ignored or closed without comment.

* tmux version (`tmux -V`).
tmux 3.7c
* Platform (`uname -sp`).
CYGWIN_NT-10.0-19045 unknown
* Host OS
Windows 10 Pro 22H2 x64
* Terminal in use (xterm, rxvt, etc).
mintty
* $TERM *inside* tmux (`echo $TERM`).
tmux-256color
* $TERM *outside* tmux (`echo $TERM`).
xterm-256color
* Logs from tmux (`tmux kill-server; tmux -vv new`).
[tmux-logs.zip](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/29932158/tmux-logs.zip)

title: Status line blanks on terminal resize in tmux 3.7 (regression — 3.6b is fine)
author: Alan-John-Byrne
body:
### Summary

On tmux **3.7b**, resizing the terminal **blanks the entire status line** — every window/tab name (and the whole `status-left` / window-list / `status-right`) disappears, leaving only the status-line background colour. It stays blank until the next *full* redraw (another resize, or switching the outer terminal's tab).

Crucially, tmux's internal model is unaffected the whole time:

- `tmux list-windows` and `tmux display-message -p '#{W:#{window_index}:#{window_name} }'` report every window and name correctly.
- `tmux refresh-client` (and `set -g status off; set -g status on; refresh-client`) is a **no-op** — tmux believes the status line is already drawn, so it never re-emits it, but the terminal's actual surface is blank.

This is a **regression in the 3.7 line**: the identical reproduction on **tmux 3.6b never blanks.**

### Version bisect (same machine, same config, same trigger)

Automated head-to-head with `status-position top`, 8 resize cycles per version, auto-detecting whether the status row rendered any glyphs (pixel analysis of a screenshot of the actual framebuffer):

| tmux build | blank status after resize |
|---|---|
| **3.7b** (`tmux-3.7b-2.fc44`) | **8 / 8** |
| **3.6b** (from the 3.6b release tarball, `./configure && make`) | **0 / 8** |

3.7b fails **deterministically** on a resize trigger; 3.6b is completely clean. (A *new-window* trigger, by contrast, does not reliably fire it on 3.7b — resize is the reliable trigger.)

### Reproduce

```sh
tmux -L t -f /dev/null new-session -d
tmux -L t set -g status-position top
tmux -L t new-window \; new-window \; new-window
tmux -L t attach
# Now resize the terminal window (maximize/restore is the most reliable).
# The window list in the status line goes blank; `tmux -L t list-windows`
# still lists them all.
```

### Environment

- **tmux 3.7b** — Fedora 44 package `tmux-3.7b-2.fc44`. Reproduced on 3.7b; **not** on 3.6b.
- Guest: **WSL2 (Fedora Linux 44)**. Outer terminal: reproduced **identically in both WezTerm and Windows Terminal** on Windows — i.e. terminal-independent.
- Verified it is a genuine tmux render and not a display/transport artifact: a **server-side framebuffer capture** shows the status row blank while the pane content renders normally in the very same frame.

### Notes

- Primarily characterised with `status-position top` (deterministic on resize); also observed blank intermittently with `bottom`.
- The environment is a software-rendered terminal over a remote session, so there may be a redraw-timing element — but the **version bisect isolates it to tmux**: nothing changes between the two runs except the tmux binary.
- Happy to **git-bisect the exact commit between 3.6b and 3.7b** if that would help narrow it down — just say the word.

Thanks for tmux — it's superb software.

title: When `pane-border-lines` is `none` and there is no tiled layout, the 'background' is painted black.

Reproduction:

title: Terminal syntax highlighting | Color of commented lines of left pane propagated to right pane
author: jer-2782
body: [tmux-server-34481.log](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/29907420/tmux-server-34481.log) [tmux-client-34479.log](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/29907419/tmux-client-34479.log) [tmux-out-34481.log](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/29907421/tmux-out-34481.log) ### Issue description Hi, I am using syntax highlighting for the SSH session to put color on 'standard' keywords (warning, error, Ok, IPv4..etc.) In particular, displaying a file that contains commented lines make them appear in green Doing this on a left pane makes the lines on right pane changing color as well (= it is the same line basically) = the green 'commented' color is propagated to the right pane while it should be only bound to left pane Easier to figure out with the screenshot I think it used to work fine before but cannot recall with which version... Thanks ### Required information $ tmux -V tmux next-3.8 8f27092 $ uname -sp Linux x86_64 $TERM inside $ echo $TERM screen-256color $TERM outside $ echo $TERM xterm-256color

title: Explicitly setting an empty window name is not respected in 3.7
author: jacobkoziej
body:
### Issue description

Following the addition of https://github.com/tmux/tmux/commit/64e83caf04944f9a45113c32ecaff588a951fb99, creating windows with empty names with `new-window -n ''` no longer works and falls back to the default window name.

### Required information

* tmux version: `tmux 3.7b`
* Platform: `Linux unknown`
* Terminal in use: <https://alacritty.org/>
* `$TERM` *inside* tmux: `tmux-256color`
* `$TERM` *outside* tmux: `xterm-256color`
* Logs:
  [tmux-client-2695.log](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/29830005/tmux-client-2695.log)
  [tmux-server-2697.log](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/29830000/tmux-server-2697.log)
  [tmux-out-2697.log](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/29830007/tmux-out-2697.log)

### Proposed Fix

```diff
diff --git a/spawn.c b/spawn.c
index e0cac4bc..af6e03d5 100644
--- a/spawn.c
+++ b/spawn.c
@@ -177,7 +177,7 @@ spawn_window(struct spawn_context *sc, char **cause)
        /* Set the name of the new window. */
        if (~sc->flags & SPAWN_RESPAWN) {
                free(w->name);
-               if (sc->name == NULL || *sc->name == '\0')
+               if (sc->name == NULL)
                        w->name = default_window_name(w);
                else {
                        w->name = xstrdup(sc->name);
```

title: Middle click doesn't work properly in tmux
author: svitalsky
body:
### Issue description

Using mouse middle click to copy and paste text doesn't work properly: when double clicking on a text to select it within tmux, it only blips in different colour (my normal would be green, this is orange) and the text can only be pasted inside the tmux, not elsewhere, and when I select a text elsewhere (std. terminal, gVim…), middle clicking within tmux does not paste it.

The offending commit seems to be (git bisect) commit 29b2b7a8754ea5656be15a49443280e6172183cb (Mouse on by default.).

My current top commit (master) is commit db115c6b97b06103a76934fb0e4abf588a6ae318.

### Required information

Please provide the following information. These are **required**. Note that bug reports without logs may be ignored or closed without comment.

* tmux version (`tmux -V`). tmux next-3.8
* Platform (`uname -sp`). Linux Manjaro
* Terminal in use (xterm, rxvt, etc). mate terminal, tilda
* $TERM *inside* tmux (`echo $TERM`). screen.xterm-256color
* $TERM *outside* tmux (`echo $TERM`). xterm-256color
* Logs from tmux (`tmux kill-server; tmux -vv new`). Attached.

[tmux-logs.tar.gz](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/29781748/tmux-logs.tar.gz)

### Note
What I meant with my previous comment about beta-testers was this: does no one test it at all? Is there no peer review or anything? How does thing like this (mouse middle click not working properly) or for example previously the absurd exclusion of colons from names even get through to master?
Please do not be mistaken, I do appreciate the work you are doing on this and I am grateful, it just seems too many of rather silly bugs get through that might have been quite easily caught with some tests or basic attention (colons!!! really?).

title: "Create a new pane to copy" recipe needs updating for tmux 3.7
author: scfarley
body:
### Issue description

In the recipe for [Create a new pane to copy](https://github.com/tmux/tmux/wiki/Recipes#create-a-new-pane-to-copy), the empty argument at the end of the `splitw` command is unnecessary.  Additionally, it no longer works with tmux 3.7b but did with tmux 3.6b.  The recipe will flash the output in a new window then the window will exit immediately.

I tested using a bare configuration file with both Zsh and FreeBSD's /bin/sh to rule out any funny business.
```sh
tmux -L recipe kill-server
tmux -L recipe -f pane-to-copy.conf
```

`pane-to-copy.conf`:
```conf
bind C {
	splitw -f -l30% ''
	set-hook -p pane-mode-changed 'if -F "#{!=:#{pane_mode},copy-mode}" "kill-pane"'
	copy-mode -s'{last}'
}
```

If a GitHub Wiki could take a PR, here is a patch that works for 3.7b.  I also tested with 3.6b, and it works with it too.
```
diff --git a/Recipes.md b/Recipes.md
index 1eaba09..1bd162c 100644
--- a/Recipes.md
+++ b/Recipes.md
@@ -75,7 +75,7 @@ multiple items from the history to the shell prompt.
 
 ```tmux
 bind C {
-	splitw -f -l30% ''
+	splitw -f -l30%
 	set-hook -p pane-mode-changed 'if -F "#{!=:#{pane_mode},copy-mode}" "kill-pane"'
 	copy-mode -s'{last}'
 }
```

### Required information

* tmux version:  `tmux 3.7b`
* Platform: `FreeBSD amd64`
* Terminal:  `xterm`
* $TERM *inside* tmux:  `tmux-256color`
* $TERM *outside* tmux:  `xterm-256color`
* Logs from tmux (`tmux kill-server; tmux -vv new`).

title: SIGSEGV in sort_qsort when a session is destroyed with detach-on-destroy previous or next
author: kylesnowschwartz
body:
### Issue description

In tmux 3.7 and later, destroying a session while `detach-on-destroy` is `previous` or `next` segfaults the server. All sessions are lost.

Reproduced on 3.7b and on master at db115c6b built from git:

```sh
tmux -Ltest -f /dev/null new-session -d -s a
tmux -Ltest set -g detach-on-destroy previous
tmux -Ltest new-session -d -s b
tmux -Ltest kill-session -t b
tmux -Ltest list-sessions
# no server running on /private/tmp/tmux-502/test
```

Killing the last pane of a session's last window crashes the same way, through `server_kill_pane`.

For `previous` and `next`, `server_destroy_session` passes NULL as the sort criteria: [server-fn.c#L449-L451](https://github.com/tmux/tmux/blob/db115c6b97b06103a76934fb0e4abf588a6ae318/server-fn.c#L449-L451). The NULL reaches `sort_qsort` via [session.c#L308](https://github.com/tmux/tmux/blob/db115c6b97b06103a76934fb0e4abf588a6ae318/session.c#L308) and is dereferenced at [sort.c#L35](https://github.com/tmux/tmux/blob/db115c6b97b06103a76934fb0e4abf588a6ae318/sort.c#L35).

Crash stack from the macOS crash report:

```
EXC_BAD_ACCESS KERN_INVALID_ADDRESS at 0x0000000000000000
sort_qsort + 0
sort_get_sessions + 148
session_previous_session + 64
server_destroy_session + 340
cmd_kill_session_exec + 548
cmdq_fire_command + 32
cmdq_next + 1436
server_loop + 100
```

3.6b is not affected: `session_previous_session` there takes no sort argument ([session.c#L313 at 3.6b](https://github.com/tmux/tmux/blob/8f3f14f565d3dc2a1e7f8c37e0dc3d3499c70c97/session.c#L313)).

A possible fix is a NULL check in `sort_qsort`, or passing a `sort_criteria` with `SORT_END` from `server_destroy_session`.

### Required information

* tmux version: 3.7b, and master at db115c6b built from git (`tmux next-3.8`)
* Platform: Darwin arm (macOS 15)
* Terminal in use: Ghostty
* $TERM inside tmux: `tmux-256color`
* $TERM outside tmux: `xterm-ghostty`
* Log: server started with `-vv`; the log ends while the `kill-session` above is being processed:

```
1783466246.376453 cmd_find_target: target b is session b
1783466246.376455 cmd_find_get_session: b
1783466246.376457 cmd_find_target: s=$1 b
1783466246.376458 cmd_find_target: wl=0 1 w=@1 tmux
1783466246.376460 cmd_find_target: wp=%1
1783466246.376462 cmd_find_target: idx=none
```

title: Theme reporting (2031/DSR 996) ignores the terminal's reported theme and always guesses from background colour
author: aymanbagabas
body:
When a pane inside tmux queries the colour scheme (`CSI ? 996 n`) or receives an unsolicited mode 2031 notification, tmux answers with a value computed from the background colour luminance, even when the outer terminal has itself reported its theme via `CSI ? 997 ; Ps n`. The terminal's authoritative value is discarded, so tmux can report the opposite of what the terminal reports.

### Environment

- tmux master (next-3.8), also present in 3.7.
- Outer terminal: any terminal that supports mode 2031 / DSR 996 (e.g. Ghostty), i.e. one that answers `CSI ? 996 n` with its own light/dark preference.

### What happens

tmux does query the outer terminal:

- `tty.c:377` sends `\e[?2031h\e[?996n` when the terminal supports it.
- The reply `\e[?997;1n` / `\e[?997;2n` is parsed in `tty-keys.c:219-220` into `KEYC_REPORT_DARK_THEME` / `KEYC_REPORT_LIGHT_THEME`, and `server_client_report_theme()` stores it in `c->theme`.

So the client theme correctly reflects the terminal. But the value a pane receives comes from `window_pane_get_theme()` (`window.c:2420`), which prioritises the background colour:

```
/* Derive theme from pane background color, if it's not the default colour. */
theme = colour_totheme(window_pane_get_bg(wp));
if (theme != THEME_UNKNOWN)
    return (theme);
/* ... only now fall back to a client that has a theme (c->theme) ... */
```

For a default pane, `window_pane_get_bg()` (`window.c:2324`) does not return "default". It falls through to `window_get_bg_client()` (`window.c:2342`), which returns the attached client's `tty.bg`, i.e. the terminal background queried over OSC 11. `colour_totheme()` (`colour.c:347`) then classifies it purely by luminance (`r+g+b > 382 => light`).

Because the terminal always answers OSC 11, `tty.bg` is essentially always set, so the `c->theme` branch (the value the terminal actually reported via 997) is never reached. The same background-first fallback is also used for the client theme itself in `server_client_update_theme_colours()` (`server-client.c:1197`), but there it is only used when `c->theme` is `THEME_UNKNOWN`, which is the correct precedence. `window_pane_get_theme()` has the precedence reversed.

### Result

- Query the terminal directly: `printf "\e[?996n"` returns the terminal's OS/config scheme.
- Query tmux running in the same terminal: tmux returns the luminance of the background colour.

These disagree whenever the background's luminance does not match the declared scheme, for example a light scheme with a slightly dark-tinted background, or any custom `background` set in the terminal that crosses the threshold the other way. Apps that use 2031/996 (delta, vim, etc.) then see the wrong theme inside tmux.

### Expected

The terminal is the authority. When the outer terminal has reported a theme via `997` (mode 2031 / DSR 996), tmux should report that value. The background-colour luminance guess should only be used as a fallback for terminals that do not support the sequence, which is what the original change intended (see #4353: "guess the theme from the background colour on terminals which do not themselves support the escape sequence").

### Suggested fix

In `window_pane_get_theme()`, prefer a known client theme (`c->theme`, from the `997` report) and only fall back to `colour_totheme(window_pane_get_bg(wp))` when no client reports a theme, mirroring the precedence already used in `server_client_update_theme_colours()`.

title: format s/// substitution with an anchored pattern drops the first character when the match is empty
author: undont

title: Synchronized output (DECSET 2026): screen content written after ED inside a sync block is not flushed to the client
author: gan4dd
body:
## Environment
- tmux 3.7a (Homebrew), macOS
- Outer terminals: Ghostty and iTerm2 (both reproduce)
- Reproduces with `tmux -f /dev/null` (no user config)

## Reproduction
Run inside a tmux pane:

```sh
printf '\e[?2026h\e[H\e[2J'
seq 1 30
printf 'frame 0\n'
printf '\e[?2026l'
```

Expected: numbers 1–30 and `frame 0` visible (as happens outside tmux).

Actual: only the last line (`frame 0`) is visible on the client. The
content *is* in the pane grid — `tmux capture-pane -p` shows all of it,
and `tmux refresh-client` makes everything appear — so only the flush
to the client is lost.

## Notes
- Without the ED (`\e[2J`), i.e. a plain sync-wrapped write, output
  displays correctly. Without the sync wrapping, the same clear+redraw
  displays correctly. The combination triggers it.
- A single sync block suffices; no repetition or timing needed.
- `#{synchronized_output_flag}` polled at 5 Hz stays 0 afterwards, so
  the pane is not stuck in sync mode; it looks like the client-diff /
  damage computation after the deferred flush treats the post-clear
  content as already displayed.
- Likely related to the app-side DECSET 2026 handling added in #4744.

## Real-world impact
Claude Code ≥ 2.1.200 wraps its initial paint (which includes a clear)
in DECSET 2026 when it detects tmux 3.4+, so its startup screen renders
blank inside tmux 3.7a until any redraw (keypress, SIGWINCH, focus
change, `refresh-client`). Reported there as anthropics/claude-code#74122.

title: scrollbar is missing
author: XhstormR
body: ### Issue description With the same configuration, the scrollbar displays correctly in version 3.7, but it disappears after upgrading to version 3.7b. Minimal configuration: ``` set -g pane-scrollbars on ``` scrollbar is gone: Update: After entering tmux, I have to manually reload the tmux configuration for the scrollbar to appear, and this only works within the current window; the scrollbar disappears when switching to another window. ``` tmux source-file ~/.tmux.conf ``` ### Required information Please provide the following information. These are **required**. Note that bug reports without logs may be ignored or closed without comment. * tmux version (`tmux -V`). 3.7b * Platform (`uname -sp`). Darwin 25.5.0 arm64 * Terminal in use (xterm, rxvt, etc). Ghostty 1.3.2-main-+b213a72c0 * $TERM *inside* tmux (`echo $TERM`). tmux-256color * $TERM *outside* tmux (`echo $TERM`). xterm-ghostty * Logs from tmux (`tmux kill-server; tmux -vv new`).

title: display-popup repaints/flickers when a background pane redraws its full region (scroll / alternate-screen) — 3.7
author: noamsto
body:
tmux 3.7b

Since 3.7, an open `display-popup` is repainted whenever a pane behind it redraws a region that overlaps the popup. On 3.6a this never happened while a popup was open.

It's easy to see the boundary: a background pane that only *appends* lines below the popup does not flicker — but as soon as its output **scrolls** (passes the pane height, shifting the rows behind the popup), the popup repaints on every scroll. A fullscreen / **alternate-screen** program (vim, btop, or any TUI) redraws its whole pane each frame, so the popup flickers continuously.

This looks like the fix for #4920 ("popup window gets overwritten by background updates") firing on every full-region background update, not only when the popup is genuinely overwritten.

**Repro (3.7b):**
1. `tmux new-session`
2. In the pane: `while :; do date +%s.%N; sleep 0.1; done`
3. `tmux display-popup -w 60% -h 60% -E 'sleep 60'`
4. No flicker while output stays below the popup; **flicker begins once the pane scrolls.** An alt-screen TUI in step 2 (e.g. `btop`) flickers immediately.

**Expected:** open popup stays static (3.6a behavior).
**Actual:** popup repaints on every overlapping background redraw.

Terminal: kitty (`xterm-kitty`), synchronized output (`sync`) enabled — flicker persists with `sync` on.

Could the popup be repainted only over the region actually overwritten (or an option to keep background panes from repainting an open popup)?
